title: How to Set Up Judge0 CE on Your Server
subtitle: A step-by-step guide to deploying your own code execution engine in minutes.
excerpt: A step-by-step guide to deploying your own code execution engine in minutes. Learn how to safely run arbitrary user-submitted code on your server.

author.name: Luban Rahat
author.bio: Building scalable systems and teaching others how to do the same.

content:
If you've ever built a coding platform, an online judge, or an automated assessment tool, you know the hardest part isn't the frontend — it's safely running arbitrary user-submitted code on your server. That's exactly what Judge0 CE solves.

In this guide, we'll walk through everything you need to get Judge0 CE v1.13.1 up and running on your own Linux server from scratch.

## What You'll Need

Before we dive in, make sure you have the following ready:

- A Linux server running **Ubuntu 22.04** (recommended)
- **Docker** and **Docker Compose** installed
- sudo access to your server

Judge0 has only been tested on Linux, so Windows and macOS are not supported for production deployments.

## Step 1 — Prepare Your Server

Judge0 relies on Linux cgroups for sandboxing user code. On Ubuntu 22.04, you need to make a small but important change to your GRUB configuration before anything else.

Open the GRUB config file:

```bash
sudo nano /etc/default/grub
```

Find the line that starts with `GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX` and add the following value inside the quotes:

```
systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0
```

Save the file, apply the changes, and reboot:

```bash
sudo update-grub
sudo reboot
```

This ensures that Isolate — the sandboxing engine Judge0 uses under the hood — has proper access to cgroup controls. Skipping this step will cause submissions to fail silently.

## Step 2 — Download Judge0

Once your server is back online, download and extract the latest release:

```bash
wget https://github.com/judge0/judge0/releases/download/v1.13.1/judge0-v1.13.1.zip
unzip judge0-v1.13.1.zip
```

## Step 3 — Configure Passwords

Judge0 uses both Redis and PostgreSQL internally. Before starting any services, you need to set strong random passwords for both.

Visit https://www.random.org/passwords/?num=1&len=32&format=plain&rnd=new to generate a password, then open `judge0.conf` and set:

```
REDIS_PASSWORD=your_generated_password_here
```

Generate a second password the same way and set:

```
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=your_second_generated_password_here
```

Never skip this step. Leaving default or empty passwords on a public-facing server is a serious security risk.

## Step 4 — Start the Services

Navigate into the extracted folder and bring up the services in the correct order:

```bash
cd judge0-v1.13.1
docker-compose up -d db redis
sleep 10s
docker-compose up -d
sleep 5s
```

The sleep commands matter here. Judge0's API server and workers need the database and Redis to be fully initialized before they start. Rushing this step is one of the most common causes of a broken first deployment.

## Step 5 — Verify Your Instance

Once everything is up, open your browser and visit:

```
http://<YOUR SERVER IP>:2358/docs
```

You should see the Judge0 API documentation page. If you can load it, your instance is running correctly and ready to accept submissions.

## Optional — Enable HTTPS

Running Judge0 behind HTTPS is strongly recommended for any public-facing deployment. Judge0 provides a separate release package with Let's Encrypt support built in.

Download the HTTPS release:

```bash
wget https://github.com/judge0/judge0/releases/download/v1.13.1/judge0-v1.13.1-https.zip
unzip judge0-v1.13.1-https.zip
cd judge0-v1.13.1-https
```

Open `docker-compose.yml` and update these three variables with your domain details:

```yaml
VIRTUAL_HOST=your.domain.com
LETSENCRYPT_HOST=your.domain.com
LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL=you@youremail.com
```

Then start the services exactly as before:

```bash
docker-compose up -d db redis
sleep 10s
docker-compose up -d
sleep 5s
```

Your instance will now be available at `https://your.domain.com` with a valid SSL certificate automatically provisioned and renewed by Let's Encrypt.

## You're Live

That's all it takes. You now have a fully functional, self-hosted code execution engine running on your own infrastructure — supporting 89 programming languages, with a clean REST API, webhook support, and full control over resource limits and configuration.

From here, you can explore the `judge0.conf` file to tune CPU time limits, memory limits, queue sizes, and network access settings to match exactly what your platform needs.

Happy judging.

---

*Based on the official Judge0 CE v1.13.1 documentation.*
